title: Enable Dracut FIPS Module
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires adding the fips module to dracut and rebuilding the initramfs, which involves bootloader/kernel initialization changes. Incorrect configuration can prevent the system from booting.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; bootloader modification; FIPS mode activation; potential boot failure

title: Enable FIPS Mode
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode requires setting the kernel boot parameter fips=1, which involves modifying boot loader configuration. Applying this change on a running system is unsupported and may render the system unbootable.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; boot loader modification; kernel parameter change; potential boot failure; unsupported on existing installations

title: Set kernel parameter 'crypto.fips_enabled' to 1
triage_reason: Enabling FIPS mode on a preinstalled system is not supported; the system must be installed with the fips=1 kernel boot parameter. Changing crypto.fips_enabled at runtime will not persist and may break cryptographic services.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; System may lose cryptographic functionality; Requires reboot or reinstall to take effect; Changing FIPS mode on a running system is unsupported; Potential disruption to services relying on cryptographic modules

title: The Installed Operating System Is Vendor Supported
triage_reason: Ensuring the OS is vendor supported involves checking subscription status and enabling appropriate repositories, which does not modify authentication, security-critical files, or filesystem structures in a way that could cause lockout or break services.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; May require network access to subscription manager
attempt_2_commands: rpm -q redhat-release; dnf provides redhat-release; rpm -q rocky-release; rpm -ql rocky-release | grep -E 'release|issue'
review_feedback: The plan relies on potentially ineffective actions such as modifying system files to appear vendor-supported or attempting subscription-manager registration on a non-RHEL system. These do not confer actual vendor support or access to Red Hat security patches. To resolve the vulnerability, the system must be running a genuine vendor-supported distribution (e.g., RHEL) with an active subscription.
review_concerns: Modifying release files does not change the underlying OS vendor support status.; Registering a Rocky Linux system with subscription-manager will not grant access to RHEL repositories.; The plan does not include a definitive step to replace the OS with a vendor-supported distribution.; Attempting to misrepresent the OS may violate compliance and break tooling that depends on accurate OS identification.

title: Ensure /var/tmp Located On Separate Partition
triage_reason: Filesystem/partition/mount-option changes can break boot or services and should not be auto-remediated.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; filesystems affected; filesystems/partitioning; service disruption risk

title: Only Authorized Local User Accounts Exist on Operating System
triage_reason: Removing local user accounts can cause loss of SSH/console access or break services if a needed account is deleted inadvertently; therefore human review is required to identify which accounts are truly unauthorized before remediation.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; potential loss of SSH/console access if a needed account is removed; breaking services that rely on specific user accounts; impact on authentication/authorization

title: Set the Boot Loader Admin Username to a Non-Default Value
triage_reason: Changing the default GRUB2 superuser account name prevents use of well-known credentials (e.g., root, admin) that could be exploited for privilege escalation. Using a unique, non-default username reduces the attack surface for boot-time authentication bypass.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; filesystems affected; default credentials; privilege escalation

title: Set Boot Loader Password in grub2
triage_reason: Modifying the GRUB2 boot loader password can render the system unbootable if the password is mistyped or forgotten, requiring physical console access or rescue media to recover.
triage_estimated_impact: reboot required; authentication/authorization affected; Boot lockout due to incorrect password; Need for physical console access to recover; Potential inability to boot if GRUB config is corrupted

title: Disable the use of user namespaces
triage_reason: Setting user.max_user_namespaces=0 is a kernel sysctl change that does not affect authentication, networking, or filesystems. It can be persisted via /etc/sysctl.d and applied without reboot, making it safe to remediate automatically.
triage_estimated_impact: May break container runtimes that rely on user namespaces
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation plan follows the exact steps specified in the vulnerability description to disable the use of user namespaces by setting the kernel parameter user.max_user_namespaces to 0. The steps are safe: checking the current value is read-only, ensuring the configuration directory exists (creating if missing is harmless), writing a configuration file in the correct location is standard practice, and applying the setting via sysctl -w is the recommended method. No critical services are broken, no lockouts occur, and no data loss is expected as the changes are confined to kernel parameter adjustment through proper sysctl interfaces.

title: Elevate The SELinux Context When An Administrator Calls The Sudo Command
triage_reason: The fix adds a standard sudoers directive (TYPE=sysadm_t ROLE=sysadm_r) via a file in /etc/sudoers.d, edited with visudo which validates syntax. This follows STIG hardening patterns for sudo and is unlikely to cause lockout or system breakage when applied correctly.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Incorrect sudoers syntax could impair sudo access if visudo is bypassed; Missing SELinux type/role definitions could cause permission denials for sudoed commands

title: Prevent Unrestricted Mail Relaying
triage_reason: Setting smtpd_client_restrictions to permit_mynetworks,reject is a standard Postfix hardening step that limits mail relaying to trusted networks. It does not alter authentication, authorization, or critical system files, and only requires a service reload.
triage_estimated_impact: networking affected; If mynetworks is incorrectly defined, legitimate mail may be blocked; Postfix service must be reloaded/restarted for changes to take effect
attempt_1_commands: bash -c 'if ! grep -q ^smtpd_client_restrictions /etc/postfix/main.cf; then echo "smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject" >> /etc/postfix/main.cf; else sed -i "s/^smtpd_client_restrictions.*/smtpd_client_restrictions = permit_mynetworks,reject/g" /etc/postfix/main.cf; fi'; systemctl reload postfix

title: Ensure Chrony is only configured with the server directive
triage_reason: The rule only requires editing the Chrony configuration file to ensure time sources are specified with the 'server' directive. This change does not affect authentication, authorization, filesystems, or require a reboot, and follows standard hardening practices for NTP configuration.
attempt_1_commands: sed -i 's/^pool /server /' /etc/chrony.conf; grep -n '^pool' /etc/chrony.conf; grep -n 'pool' /etc/chrony.conf; grep -n '^peer' /etc/chrony.conf; grep -n '^server' /etc/chrony.conf; systemctl restart chronyd; grep -n '^server' /etc/chrony.conf
review_feedback: Plan changes pool to server and verifies no other pool/peer directives, then restarts chronyd.
review_concerns: Changing from pool to server may reduce NTP source redundancy and load balancing.
qa_verdict_reason: Changing pool to server satisfies the directive-only requirement; restarting chronyd is a normal operation and unlikely to break critical services.
qa_side_effects: reduced NTP source redundancy; potentially less optimal time synchronization

title: Enable Certmap in SSSD
triage_reason: Enabling certmap in SSSD involves adding a configuration section to /etc/sssd/sssd.conf. This is a standard authentication hardening step that does not modify critical boot, partitioning, or firewall settings. If misconfigured, it may cause authentication issues but can be reverted by restoring the original config.
triage_estimated_impact: authentication/authorization affected; Incorrect certmap syntax could cause SSSD authentication failures; Service restart required; temporary interruption of SSSD-dependent logins
review_feedback: Plan adds certmap configuration but uses a generic matchrule that may be overly permissive; should be tailored to environment.
review_concerns: Generic matchrule may map any certificate SAN to a user, potentially causing unintended authorization.
qa_verdict_reason: The remediation only adds a certmap section to /etc/sssd/sssd.conf, which is a reversible configuration change that does not modify critical services such as sshd, auditd, firewalld, or networking.
qa_side_effects: Overly permissive certificate mapping (matchrule = <SAN>.* ) could unintentionally allow any certificate to map to a user, leading to potential authorization issues.
